title: Mum Guilt: What It Is and What to Do with It
subtitle: A clear-eyed reading on the two kinds of guilt mothers carry, and how to respond to each
excerpt: A reading on mum guilt that distinguishes the useful kind, the guilt that signals a real misalignment between your values and your actions, from the background guilt that is structural, cultural, and cannot be resolved by doing more. Each kind has a different response.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education, drawing on the research on maternal guilt and moral emotion including Wendy Hollway and Tony Jefferson's work on anxiety and subjectivity, and Brené Brown's distinction between guilt and shame in Daring Greatly (2012).

content.text: Mum guilt is a recognisable experience: the chronic low-level sense of not quite doing enough, of falling short of some mothering standard that is never fully defined and never fully met. It is so common that it has become a cultural shorthand. That normalisation is not necessarily helpful. It tends to make the guilt feel both inevitable and irreducible, which means many women carry it without examining it.

Guilt, in its basic psychological form, is the feeling that arises when your actions are misaligned with your values. It is a moral emotion: it says something went wrong. The distinction researchers and therapists make is between guilt about a behaviour (I did something I believe was wrong) and shame about identity (I am bad). Guilt about behaviour is workable. Shame about identity tends to make things worse.

Most mum guilt is not guilt about a specific behaviour. It is a diffuse sense of insufficiency that does not attach to a single act and cannot be resolved by correcting one. This is not guilt in the functional sense. It is anxiety, or it is internalised cultural pressure, or it is exhaustion wearing guilt as a costume.
The useful kind and the not-useful kind

The useful guilt: you shouted when you didn't mean to, or you made a specific choice you know you'd make differently. It points to something real and actionable. You can apologise, repair, and adjust. This guilt is a signal worth listening to.

The not-useful guilt: you went to the gym, or you enjoyed an evening alone, or you fed them something convenient on a hard day, or you went back to work, and something in you registered it as wrong. This is not moral information. It is the voice of a standard you did not choose and cannot meet. Treating it as a signal, by doing more, sacrificing more, reducing yourself further, does not make it stop. It escalates.
Why it tends to be gendered

The research on parental guilt shows it falls unevenly. Mothers carry far more long-term guilt than fathers in the same parenting situations. This is true across cultures. The reason is not biological. It comes from a cultural frame in which mothering is treated as an identity to perform well or badly, rather than a relationship that is always going to be imperfect.

The culture provides a continuous standard, the good mother, that shifts to ensure it cannot quite be met. When women worked outside the home less, the standard emphasised presence. When women work, the standard emphasises enrichment and emotional attunement. The standard is not about the children. It is a mechanism for keeping maternal labour at maximum output.
What to do with the useful kind

When guilt is pointing at something real: name the behaviour specifically, repair it where possible, and decide what you would do differently. Then finish. Guilt that has been addressed does not need to be carried indefinitely. If you find yourself continuing to feel guilty after a real repair, that is shame wearing guilt's clothes, and shame needs a different response.
What to do with the rest

The diffuse background guilt requires a harder thing: the decision not to act on it. Not suppression, which tends to amplify it. But refusal to treat it as reliable moral information. A practical question that helps: what is the guilt actually asking me to give up, and do I believe my children actually need me to give that up? In many cases the answer is no. The guilt was asking for a sacrifice that would serve neither the children nor the mother.
Written for homemade.education. Draws on Brené Brown's work in her book Daring Greatly on the gap between guilt and shame. Also draws on research into maternal guilt and gendered parenting.
